Subiecr: Inquiry of Elwayne E. Smith, Citl, Attorney, Huntington
Park, in his Letter of September 13' 1976
I talked with Mr. Smith on September 23 about each of the
three questions in his letter. 
The fi.rst two questions involve
the situation of a City Councilman whose three-par golf course
concessj,on corporation has a contract with an adjoining city or
county. Mr. smith asked if there is any conflict with the cor-
poration in question acting with respect to matters before the
-ity Council involving the relationship between the City of
Hunlington Park with the adjoining city or county. He was
informed that the answer is no -- no conflict of interest exists
between two public entities, and so long as the actions of the
Huntington Park city Council did not involve the ratification
or enhincement of the value of the contracts i'n guestion, there
is no possibilitY of a conflict.
Mr. Smith's third question involved the situation in which
he, as City Attorney, represents mernbers of the City Council
wiih respelt to their Private affaj-rs, unrelated to the Counci'l-
men's official duties. llr. Smith inquired whether or not he
cou,ld participate in advising the city council on normal council
matter! in such a situation. 
I informed him that if, in fact,
the amount paid to him as City Atiorney rendered the councilman
2s a source of income to him, he would be required to disgualify
himself only on those matters which had a material financial
effect upon the source of income, namely, the City Councilman.
vle agreed that the potential for these situations arising was
extr6mely remote. As a rule of thumb, I advised him that when
City Councilmen who $rere sources of inct-,ne to him disqualified
theisetves with respect to a matter, he should consider disqual-
ifying himself from participating in- the matter before the city
Coirncit, tle agreed that when these kinds of situations arose,
he would get back to us with further fact patterns and not deal
with the situation in hypothetical fashion.

. Does this councilnan have a conflict of

sections.conclusion: First two questions: No conflict of interest exists between two public entities, and so long as City Council actions do not involve ratification or enhancement of contract values, there is no conflict possibility. Third question: If the amount paid to the City Attorney by a councilman renders that councilman a source of income, the attorney must disqualify himself only on matters with material financial effects on that income source. When councilmen who are income sources disqualify themselves, the attorney should consider disqualifying himself as well.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a city councilman have conflicts of interest when his corporations hold concession contracts with adjacent municipalities, and can the city attorney represent individual councilmembers on private matters while advising the council?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: No conflict of interest exists between two public entities regarding the golf course concessions, provided city council actions do not enhance contract values. The city attorney may represent councilmembers on private matters but must disqualify himself from council matters that materially affect councilmembers who are his income sources.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (dated Sept 13, 1976) and the FPPC memorandum response (dated Sept 28, 1976). OCR quality is moderate with some character recognition issues. Verbatim question and conclusion sections extracted from both documents. The response is based on a phone conversation rather than formal written advice.

QUESTION: . Does this councilnan have a conflict of
interest in any transactj.ons between the County of Los
Jtngeles antl the City of Huntington Park, such as contracts
for road improvement funds, contracts dealing with
housing and conmunity devel-opment projects administereal
by the qounty, joint use of County-City facilitieg in
the Civic center, contracts with the County for certain
tlt)es of services and similar transactions?
3. 
Facts. 
I am City Attorney on a part time basis and
mai-ntain a separate and private office from the office
provided at the city HaIL on occasion in the past. r
have performed legaL services for inalivj-alual nenbers of
the City Council, who have indivialually paid ne for
such services, rrhich have nothing to alo $'ith the councilmanr s
official duties.
would it be a conflict of interest for me
-to 
continue to perform 1e9al- services for individual
councilmen for private 1ega1 matters, where I woultl
receive more than $250.00 per year for such services?
Your response to the above questions rrould be appreciated at
the earliest opportunity, If a different procedure is

me,
Yours very truly,
Elwayne E. Srnith
City Attorney
EES,/dr

CONCLUSION: First two questions: No conflict of interest exists between two public entities, and so long as City Council actions do not involve ratification or enhancement of contract values, there is no conflict possibility. Third question: If the amount paid to the City Attorney by a councilman renders that councilman a source of income, the attorney must disqualify himself only on matters with material financial effects on that income source. When councilmen who are income sources disqualify themselves, the attorney should consider disqualifying himself as well.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to City Attorney Elwayne Smith's inquiry regarding potential conflicts of interest involving a councilman's golf course concession corporations with adjacent jurisdictions and the city attorney's representation of individual councilmembers on private legal matters. The FPPC determined that no conflicts exist between the public entities and outlined disqualification requirements for the city attorney based on material financial impact.
